titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Owen Luck, Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii, British Columbia
Luck (Owen), Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii, British Columbia
titlestmt.author: by Matthew Daniel Mason

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 20, 2019 at 04:01

did.unittitle: Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii, British Columbia

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii (formerly known as the Queen Charlotte Islands), British Columbia, 2011-2012, printed as thirty-three black-and-white gelatin silver prints and five color inkjet prints that chiefly document Haida and Tlingit people, sites, and cultures.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

bioghist.head: Owen Luck (born 1947)
bioghist.p: Owen Luck is an American photographer known for his photography of Native American and First Nation Canadian life and history.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Owen Luck, Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii, British Columbia, is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Owen Luck on the Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2013.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Owen Luck, Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii, British Columbia. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs of Alaska and Haida Gwaii (formerly known as the Queen Charlotte Islands), British Columbia, 2011-2012, printed as thirty-three black-and-white gelatin silver prints and five color inkjet prints that chiefly document Haida and Tlingit people, sites, and cultures.

Images of Haida people and sites in 2011 include the installation of totem poles at Hydaburg, Alaska, and views of Ninstints, a historic Haida village site. Portraits of Haida include Crystal Robinson, Darin Swanson, Mary Swanson, Candace White, and Christian White.

Images of Tlingit people and sites include the installation of totem poles at Kalwock, Alaska, in 2011, and a celebration in Juneau, Alaska, in 2012. Identified Tlingit include members of dance groups including the Heinyaa Khwaan Dance Group led by Jonathan O. Rowan, Jr.

Several images document dance groups including Haida members of the Tluu Xaada Naay (Canoe Peoples House) Dance Group from Massett, British Columbia, and Tsimshian members of the Git-Hoan (People of the Salmon) Native Dance Group from Metlakatla, Alaska.
Signed, numbered, and dated by the photographer on versos of prints. Arranged by the photographer.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four series: I. Black-and-White Photographs, 11 x 14 Inches (28 x 36 centimeters), 2011. II. Black-and-White Photographs, 16 x 20 Inches (41 x 51 centimeters), 2011. III. Black-and-White Photographs, 20 x 24 Inches (51 x 61 centimeters), 2011. IV. Color Inkjet Prints, 17 x 23 Inches (43 x 60 centimeters), 2011-2012.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

subject.part: Indians of North America -- Pictorial works
Totem poles -- Alaska -- Hydaburg -- Pictorial works
Totem poles -- Alaska -- Klawock -- Pictorial works
Totem poles -- British Columbia -- Ninstints Site -- Pictorial works

did.unittitle: Black-and-White Photographs, 11 x 14 Inches (28 x 36 centimeters)

title.part: Generations Meet. The Totem Pole Raisings Take Place in Conjunction with Culture Camp where Haida Children Learn about their Culture and Art. The Poles are Carved by a few Haida Carvers.
Children Get Carried to the Pole Park
Women Carry a Totem Pole to the Park for Raising
Totem Pole Going Up
Higher . . .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Context provided by Luck for the subseries: "Jon Rowan a respected Tlingit artist teaches, Tlingit children Tlingit Art, from kindergarten until they complete high school. [The year] 2011 marked the first time in eighty years that totem poles in the Totem Pole Park, weathered and worn, were replaced. Five heraldic monuments were stored away and replaced with five new replica poles carved by Jon Rowan and his apprentices, all students before graduating to go on to college.
In 2013 five more poles will be erected. Another eighty years will go by before this event is renewed."

title.part: On a Rainy August Morning the Killer Whale Pole is the First Totem Pole to be Carried from Jon Rowan's Long House for a Rainy Three Miles to the Totem Pole Park in the Heart of Klawock
The Killer Whale Pole Moves Along . . .
Aided by a Young Tlingit Warrior, Tlingit Men Haul a Second as it Makes its Way to the Park
Straight into the Afternoon Sun of the Only Day it Did Not Rain. Tlingit Men Heft a Double Flicker Pole to its Raising Place
It Takes the Effort of Many Hands to Lift the Pole
A Tlingit Chieftain of the Flicker Clan Looks on as the Pole Celebrating his Clan is Made Fast
A Tlingit Elder Greets the Photographer
A Tlingit Family from Ketchikan, Alaska Poses
Members of Jon Rowan's Dance Group Wearing Tlingit War Regalia Carved by Jon Rowan Gather for a Photograph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Members of the Heinyaa Khwaan dance group.

title.part: Nonnie Mary Swanson, Matriarch of the Haida People

did.unittitle: Black-and-White Photographs, 16 x 20 Inches (41 x 51 centimeters)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Black-and-white photographs are gelatin silver prints.

title.part: Luck's Adoptive Haida Family. From left to right: Crystal Robinson (Mother), Darin Swanson (Uncle), Mary Swanson (Nonnie/Grandmother)
Mary Swanson, Matriarch of the Yahgu Jaanaas/Laanaas Clan, and Haida People
Darin Swanson, Chief Ginaawaan of Dadens, of the Yahgu Jaanaas/Laanaas Clan
Candace and Christian White in Christian's "Tluu Xaada Naay" Long House
Display of Christian White's Art

did.unittitle: S'gung Gwaii, United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World Heritage Site

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: S'gung Gwaii, also known as SG̱ang Gwaay Llanagaay ("Red Cod Island") or by its English name Ninstints, is a Haida village site and part of the Gwaii Haanas National Park Reserve and Haida Heritage Site on Haida Gwaii on the North Coast of British Columbia, Canada. The site became a United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World Heritage Site in 1981.

Context provided by Luck for the subseries with a quote by Michael M. Ames, Director of the Museum of Anthropology at the University of British Columbia in the foreword to George F. MacDonald, Ninstints, Haida World Heritage Site , Museum Note No. 12 (Vancouver: University of British Columbia Press, 1983), iii: "The Kunghit Haida of Ninstints are no more -- they did not survive the onslaught of Western civilization. Their village, now only ruins, has been declared a World Heritage Site in memory of the cultivated society that once flourished there. But Ninstints is more than a celebrated relic; it provides as well a lesson in history and morals."

title.part: Sailing Along the Gwaii Haanas Coast on the Way to S'gung Gwaii
House Pit
Detail Totem Pole
Pole Detail
Pole Detail in Foreground

did.unittitle: Black-and-White Photographs, 20 x 24 Inches (51 x 61 centimeters)
S'gung Gwaii, United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World Heritage Site

title.part: Beginning Down Footpath
Looking Back Up the Trail
House Pit Looking Back at the Trail
Farther Along
Looking Back
From the Beach
Three More Magnificent Mortuary Poles
Life Goes On

did.unittitle: Color Inkjet Prints, 17 x 23 Inches (43 x 60 centimeters)

title.part: The Tlingit and Their Guests Gather in the Totem Pole Park

Jon Rowan Leads his Dancers into the Hall for the First of Three Nights of Feasting, Singing, and Dancing, as the Tlingit of Klawock, Alaska, Celebrate the Renewal of Their Culture with the Raising of Five New Poles
Killer Whale Dancer of the Git-Hoan (People of the Salmon) Native Dance Group from Metlakatla, Alaska
The Haida "Tluu Xaada Naay" dancers
Christian White Carving an Argillite Raven Head
